content:
Tender Fintech's Fake Users and Revenue: Can Your System Detect the Red Flags? | Nina Willdorf posted on the topic

𝗔𝗜 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗳𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀: 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗮 𝗺𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝘀𝗶𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗶𝗻 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗜 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗮 𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗯 𝗶𝘁 (𝘆𝗲𝘁).

Season 4 of Industry is the most-watched show on HBO right now. The central plot? Tender—a fintech built on entirely fabricated numbers. "Fake users drive fake revenue drives fake cash." The whole thing is nothing.

Harper (the show's calculating antihero) is trying to short them. Whitney (the CFO-turned-leader) is lobbying politicians for a banking license while the house of cards wobbles. It's a masterclass in failure modes that your products claim to solve.

So here's my challenge:
🔍 𝗙𝗿𝗮𝘂𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Tender's fake users and fake revenue were apparently... undetected? For years? Walk us through how your system would have flagged this. What signals? At what stage? Show your work.

📊 𝗜𝗻𝘃𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘀𝗶𝘀: Harper's doing manual detective work—sending associates to Ghana to interview vendors, piecing together clues. A short seller shouldn't need boots on the ground to figure out the company is "nothing."
How does your tool surface "the thing is nothing" before someone has to get on a plane?

👥 𝗣𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗽𝘀: The dysfunction inside Tender is textbook. Executives sleeping together for trade secrets. A CEO who's the "face" while the CFO runs the actual scheme. No one who knows what's happening saying anything. 
If you're building AI for people/talent/org health, what would your platform detect—and when?

Tender is fictional, but the patterns aren't. Wirecard was real. FTX was real. Frank was real.

If you're building AI that claims to surface truth where humans are incentivized to hide it—here's your case study. Pick a Tender problem. Show us the receipts! Put that sweet sweet UI to work.

https://lnkd.in/gNZ84u_K
title: Tender Fintech's Fake Users and Revenue: Can Your System Detect the Red Flags? | Nina Willdorf posted on the topic

content:
You have 150 milliseconds to decide if a $2,400 transaction is fraud. | Tristan McKinnon

You have 150 milliseconds to decide if a $2,400 transaction is fraud. Too slow and the customer's card gets declined at checkout. Too cautious and you lose $2,400. Welcome to real-time fraud detection.

Batch ML doesn't work here. By the time your nightly model runs and updates predictions, the fraudster has already maxed out three credit cards. You need streaming architecture: Kafka or Azure Event Hubs ingesting transaction events, Redis or DynamoDB feature stores serving pre-computed customer history (average transaction size, typical merchant categories, recent velocity), and ML models deployed at the edge making inference calls in under 50ms.

The real challenge isn't the technology, it's the business constraint. False positives cost you customer trust. I've seen fintech companies lose 20-30% of declined customers permanently because nobody wants their card rejected at dinner. False negatives cost you money directly. You need fallback rules when your model is uncertain: if customer lifetime value is high and transaction is within 2x their normal range, approve it and flag for review.

Circuit breakers matter too. When your feature store goes down at 2am, you can't just decline every transaction. You fail open with conservative rules based only on the transaction itself, accept slightly higher fraud, and page the on-call engineer.

What's harder in your experience: tuning the model to catch more fraud, or managing the operational complexity of keeping inference latency under 100ms? | 29 comments on LinkedIn
title: You have 150 milliseconds to decide if a $2,400 transaction is fraud. | Tristan McKinnon

content:
Banks Shift to Real-Time Fraud Prevention with AI and Analytics | Juan Rivera posted on the topic

Fraud is evolving faster than ever — and the best banks in the world are shifting from detection to real‑time prevention.  
The new frontier is practical, tactical, and powered by advanced analytics and AI.

Here are the strategies gaining the most traction:

1️⃣ Real‑time behavioral biometrics  
Keystroke rhythm, mouse movement, mobile pressure patterns — all analyzed in milliseconds.
This is stopping account‑takeover attempts before credentials are even used.

2️⃣ Cross‑channel intelligence  
Fraud no longer happens in silos.
Leading institutions are merging signals from payments, digital banking, call centers, and credit applications to detect anomalies that would be invisible in isolation.

3️⃣ AI‑driven anomaly detection  
Machine learning models are identifying micro‑patterns humans can’t see:
• unusual device fingerprints
• abnormal session flows
• synthetic identity clusters
• coordinated mule activity

4️⃣ Customer‑centric authentication  
The shift is clear: reduce friction for legitimate customers, increase friction for attackers.
Dynamic authentication adapts in real time based on risk level — not static rules.

5️⃣ Human + AI collaboration  
Fraud teams are using AI copilots to:
• summarize cases
• surface hidden links
• prioritize alerts
• accelerate investigations

The result: faster decisions, fewer false positives, and stronger protection.

The takeaway:  
Fraud is no longer a back‑office function — it’s a strategic capability.
The banks that win will be the ones that combine AI, behavioral intelligence, and cross‑channel orchestration to stay ahead of increasingly sophisticated threats.
title: Banks Shift to Real-Time Fraud Prevention with AI and Analytics | Juan Rivera posted on the topic

content:
"FraudTransformer: Time-Aware GPT for Fraud Detection" at ICAIF | Gholamali Aminian posted on the topic

If you’re at ICAIF this week, come along to our talk at AI-FIND workshop:
“FraudTransformer: Time-Aware GPT for Transaction Fraud Detection”

In this work, we introduce a GPT-style sequence model that uses both the order of events and the irregular time gaps between transactions to detect  frauds in real-world banking streams. On a large industrial dataset, our time-aware GPT outperforms baselines (including Logistic Regression, XGBoost and LightGBM).

📄 Paper: https://lnkd.in/dyVbKsMy

A great collaboration involving The Alan Turing Institute, HSBC and members from both organisations, Andrew Elliott, Tiger L., Timothy Wong, Victor Dehon, Carsten Maple, Lukasz Szpruch, Christopher Read, Martin Brown, Gesine Reinert, Mo Mamouei

#ICAIF #AIinFinance #FraudDetection #MachineLearning #Transformers #HSBC #TheAlanTuringInstitute
title: "FraudTransformer: Time-Aware GPT for Fraud Detection" at ICAIF | Gholamali Aminian posted on the topic

content:
#ManagingFinancialFraud with AI | Madan Pandey

#ManagingFinancialFraud with AI
AI can play a significant role in detecting, preventing, and managing financial fraud by leveraging machine learning, data analysis, and pattern recognition.

🔅Key Ways AI Helps Manage Financial Fraud:

📎Anomaly Detection: AI identifies unusual patterns or transactions that may indicate fraud.
📎Predictive Analytics: AI predicts potential fraud based on historical data and trends.
📎Real-time Monitoring: AI monitors transactions in real-time to detect and flag suspicious activity.
📎Automation of Checks: AI automates routine checks, freeing up human resources for complex investigations.

#AITechniquesforFraudManagement:
⚠️Machine Learning Models: Train models to detect fraud patterns and adapt to new tactics.
⚠️Data Analytics: Analyze large volumes of data to identify fraud indicators.
⚠️Behavioral Analysis: Monitor user behavior to detect deviations indicating potential fraud.

#BenefitsofAIinFinancialFraud Management:
👉Enhanced Detection: AI detects fraud more efficiently than traditional methods.
👉Reduced False Positives: AI improves accuracy in identifying genuine fraud cases.
👉Speed: AI enables real-time detection and response to fraud.

#Challenges:
📍Data Quality: AI effectiveness depends on data quality and availability.
📍Evolving Threats: AI models need updates to stay effective against new fraud tactics.

#ImplementingAI for Fraud Management:
✅Integrate with Existing Systems: Combine AI with current fraud management tools.
✅Continuous Learning: Update AI models regularly to adapt to new fraud patterns.

AI is a powerful tool in managing financial fraud, enhancing detection, and reducing risks

#FinancialFraudPrevention #AIinFinance #FraudDetection #MachineLearning #DataAnalytics #FinancialSecurity #CyberSecurity #RiskManagement #Fintech #ArtificialIntelligence #FraudManagement
title: #ManagingFinancialFraud with AI | Madan Pandey

content:
Banks are rushing to deploy AI for fraud detection and credit scoring, but few are asking the critical question: | Parkar Ali

Banks are rushing to deploy AI for fraud detection and credit scoring, but few are asking the critical question: 

“How’s your data governance?”

Too often, the focus is on speed and automation, not on how these systems impact customer experience (CX). Poor governance doesn’t just create compliance risk but can also lead to inconsistent decisions, customer distrust, and reputational damage.
During my experience working with several Financial Institutions, we established strict governance protocols before any ML model went into production. We built full data lineage so every prediction was traceable to its source. We automated our quality framework with bias detection built directly into the system. From day one, our Governance Council was involved to ensure transparency, accountability, and alignment with both business goals and customer trust.
The results spoke for themselves; models that were compliant, explainable and customer-centric.
Across the banking industry, I’ve noticed a consistent pattern:
Organizations with strong governance frameworks scale AI responsibly and confidently. Those with weak governance often face regulatory challenges and erode customer confidence.
AI without proper governance isn’t innovation - it’s a liability waiting to materialize.
What approach is your organization taking to AI governance?
#DataGovernance #AIStrategy #BankingLeadership #CustomerExperience #ResponsibleAI | 22 comments on LinkedIn
title: Banks are rushing to deploy AI for fraud detection and credit scoring, but few are asking the critical question: | Parkar Ali

content:
Joining Socure to tackle identity and fraud challenges | Charlie Kroll posted on the topic

People have asked why I joined Socure for this next chapter.

The honest answer is less about the company, and more about the problem.

I've worked on different parts of identity and fraud for many years, starting at Andera where we built the first digital account opening platform for banks. This was before there were orchestration platforms like Socure's RiskOS, so we had to build a lot ourselves.

When onboarding a new consumer, which data sources need to be called? In what order? Under what conditions? What determines an accept vs decline vs review decision?

At Andera, we ended up building an internal service called ARM ("Application Risk Management") which had a visual drag-and-drop editor very much like the current crop of orchestration platforms. It was incredibly powerful and allowed Andera to support bespoke onboarding workflows for 650 banks and credit unions. It was ahead of its time.

We even considered productizing it and selling it as a new product. But believe it not, back in ~2010, there was no market for a headless API platform. Banks didn't have developers, and the fintech market was a fraction of what it is today. In retrospect we missed what was coming, but that can be a story for another time.

Fast forward, the market has matured and today this is a solved problem. Socure products are available through a number of standalone orchestration providers, and Socure has its own orchestration platform called RiskOS. 

Socure's approach with RiskOS is different - vertically integrate the models with the orchestration and decisioning. This has a number of advantages in terms of performance and developer experience. Think about finally having an Apple option in an Android world, while still having the benefits of an open marketplace with hundreds of non-Socure products available. It's genuinely incredible and disruptive.

Having spent a lot of time close
title: Joining Socure to tackle identity and fraud challenges | Charlie Kroll posted on the topic

content:
Understanding Legacy Core Banking Systems and the Need for Modernization

Legacy core banking systems are the backbone of a bank's critical operations, such as managing accounts, processing transactions, handling loans, and maintaining customer data. These systems, built decades ago using outdated technologies like COBOL and mainframes, have been reliable over time.
title: Understanding Legacy Core Banking Systems and the Need for Modernization

SaaS in Core Banking - hype or reality?

Most incumbent vendors outside the US have jumped on the SaaS bandwagon in the past 5 years or so, driven by pressure from investors, analysts, cloud-native neo-vendors, business consultants, delivery partners and indeed banks themselves, especially challenger banks. All these constituents expect th
title: SaaS in Core Banking - hype or reality?
